stories.headline: NYC Doormen and Building Workers Reach Deal to Avert Strike
stories.summary:
Negotiators for approximately 34,000 New York City apartment building workers — including doormen, porters, and superintendents — reached a tentative agreement Friday with building owners, averting a strike that had threatened to disrupt residential life across the city. The deal was announced by the union representing the workers, which had been in contract talks with the Realty Advisory Board on Labor Relations.

The last time these workers staged a walkout was in 1991, making the threat of a strike a significant moment for the city's residential real estate sector. Doormen and building staff provide essential services to hundreds of thousands of residents in co-ops, condominiums, and rental buildings across the five boroughs.

Details of the tentative agreement, including wage increases and benefit provisions, were not immediately released pending ratification by union members. Both sides indicated the deal addresses core worker demands while remaining workable for building owners navigating rising operating costs.

The agreement removes what had been a looming disruption for New York City residents heading into the spring season, and signals that labor negotiations in the city's building services sector — often a bellwether for broader municipal labor trends — concluded without the work stoppage that many had feared.

stories.headline: Kanye West Concert in Poland Cancelled Over Antisemitic Comments
stories.summary:
A planned Kanye West concert at the Silesian Stadium in Chorzów, Poland has been cancelled, the venue announced Friday, citing 'formal and legal reasons.' The cancellation followed sustained pressure from Polish government officials, including Culture and Heritage Minister Marta Cienkowska, who called the decision to book West 'unacceptable' given his repeated antisemitic, racist, and pro-Nazi public statements.

The cancellation is the latest in a series of bans and postponements affecting West's European tour. The United Kingdom barred West from entering the country to headline the Wireless Festival, which was subsequently cancelled entirely. A performance in Marseille, France has been postponed indefinitely. Promoting Nazi symbols is a criminal offense in Poland, carrying a potential prison sentence of up to three years, making the country's sensitivity to West's conduct particularly acute — Chorzów was among the first towns invaded by Nazi Germany in September 1939.

West, also known as Ye, began selling swastika-branded merchandise in February 2025, prompting e-commerce platform Shopify to remove his store. He later released a track titled 'Heil Hitler' in which he linked a child custody dispute and financial difficulties to his embrace of Nazi ideology. In January 2026, ahead of his European tour announcement, he published a full-page apology in the Wall Street Journal, stating 'I am not a Nazi or an antisemite' and attributing his behavior to bipolar disorder.

Cienkowska wrote on social media that West had 'deliberately crossed boundaries' and was 'normalising hatred,' adding that culture 'cannot be a space for those who exploit it to spread hatred.' A government spokesman acknowledged before the venue's announcement that no specific Polish law directly barred the concert, but said both the culture and foreign ministries agreed it should not proceed.

stories.headline: NJ Transit to Charge $150 Round-Trip for World Cup Matches at MetLife Stadium
stories.summary:
New Jersey Transit has confirmed it will charge $150 for a round-trip ticket from New York's Penn Station to MetLife Stadium for World Cup matches this summer — an elevenfold increase over the standard fare of $12.90. The announcement has drawn widespread outrage from fans and sparked a public dispute between New Jersey Gov. Mikie Sherrill and FIFA over who bears responsibility for the cost burden.

NJ Transit officials defended the pricing, arguing the agency is not turning a profit from the surcharge and that the elevated fare reflects the logistical demands of managing massive crowds for one of the world's largest sporting events. FIFA President Gianni Infantino separately confirmed this week that Iran will participate in the World Cup despite its ongoing war with the United States.

Critics, including consumer advocates and transit riders, argue the pricing effectively prices out lower-income fans and undermines the accessibility goals of hosting a global tournament. Governor Sherrill has publicly pushed back against the pricing structure, framing it as an unfair burden placed on ordinary residents and visitors.

The controversy comes as the New York-New Jersey metro area prepares to host multiple World Cup matches at MetLife Stadium, one of the tournament's primary venues. The pricing debate adds to broader public frustration over the costs associated with major international events held in the region.

stories.headline: U.S. Deports Roughly 15 Latin American Migrants to Congo Under Third-Country Agreement
stories.summary:
Approximately 15 people deported from the United States landed in Kinshasa, the Democratic Republic of Congo, in the early hours of Friday, according to a lawyer representing one of the deportees. The individuals are all from Latin American countries, marking one of the latest instances of the Trump administration using third-country deportation agreements with African nations to accelerate migrant removals.

U.S. attorney Alma David, who represents one of the deportees, said all those removed are believed to hold legal protections from U.S. judges that shield them from being returned to their home countries. The Congolese government plans to hold the deportees temporarily, with the International Organization for Migration — a United Nations-affiliated agency — set to facilitate what officials are describing as 'assisted voluntary return' to their countries of origin.

Critics have raised serious concerns about the legality and ethics of the arrangement. 'The fact that the focus is on offering them voluntary return to their home country when they spent months in immigration detention in the U.S. fighting hard to not have to go home is very alarming,' David told the Associated Press. Rights advocates note that several African countries with which the U.S. has struck such deals — including Eswatini, South Sudan, and Equatorial Guinea — have notoriously poor human rights records.

The Trump administration has spent at least $40 million to deport approximately 300 migrants to countries other than their own, according to a recent report by Democratic staff on the Senate Foreign Relations Committee. Congo's Ministry of Communications said the arrangement reflects its 'commitment to human dignity and international solidarity' and that each case will be subject to individual review under Congolese law.

stories.headline: Update: IEA Warns Europe Has Roughly Six Weeks of Jet Fuel Left as Iran War Chokes Supply
stories.summary:
International Energy Agency Executive Director Fatih Birol warned Thursday that Europe has 'maybe six weeks or so' of jet fuel remaining, cautioning that flight cancellations could begin 'soon' if oil supplies through the Strait of Hormuz remain blocked by the Iran war. Speaking to the Associated Press from the IEA's Paris headquarters, Birol described the situation as 'the largest energy crisis we have ever faced,' driven by the near-total interruption of oil, gas, and other commodities through the strait, which in peacetime carries roughly 20 percent of the world's traded oil.

Birol said the economic pain would manifest as higher gasoline, gas, and electricity prices globally, but warned the hardest-hit nations would not necessarily be the wealthiest. 'The countries who will suffer the most will not be those whose voices are heard a lot. It will be mainly the developing countries — poorer countries in Asia, in Africa and in Latin America,' he said, adding that no nation was immune.

The warning adds urgency to ongoing U.S.-Iran diplomatic efforts, with a Pakistani delegation in Tehran this week seeking to lay groundwork for a second round of talks. Today's separate news that Iran reopened the Strait of Hormuz to commercial shipping sent oil prices sharply lower, though the IEA's alarm underscores how quickly reserves had depleted and how much damage a prolonged closure could still inflict.

The IEA chief's remarks come as European governments and airlines face mounting pressure to accelerate contingency planning, including tapping strategic reserves. Birol did not specify which countries were closest to critical shortfalls, but the breadth of the warning — encompassing aviation fuel, heating gas, and electricity generation — signals that the crisis has moved beyond an abstract geopolitical dispute into a concrete logistical emergency for consumers and governments.

stories.headline: 86-Year-Old French Widow Returns Home After ICE Detention in Alabama
stories.summary:
Marie-Thérèse, an 86-year-old French woman who was detained by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement in Alabama, arrived back in France on Friday, her family and French Foreign Minister Jean-Noël Barrot confirmed. Her son described her as 'in a state of shock and physically and spiritually exhausted' when the family was reunited at Paris-Charles de Gaulle Airport, where she was still wearing a stained and torn prison uniform.

Marie-Thérèse had moved to the United States after marrying her long-lost American love, Billy, who died in January. She was in the process of applying for a green card when ICE arrested her the day before a scheduled legal hearing. The U.S. Department of Homeland Security said she had overstayed her 90-day visa, while her son said she was lawfully awaiting permanent residency documentation. ICE agents handcuffed her hands and feet at the time of the arrest, her son told French media.

The case drew sharp attention in France and became a flashpoint in the broader debate over the Trump administration's immigration enforcement policies. Since taking office, President Trump has significantly expanded ICE's budget and mandate, directing the agency to pursue mass deportations. French officials publicly demanded her release and Foreign Minister Barrot expressed satisfaction at her return, while also pressing Washington on the methods used during her detention.

Supporters of the administration's immigration enforcement argue that visa overstays must be treated uniformly under the law regardless of personal circumstances, and that border integrity requires consistent enforcement. Critics contend the detention of an elderly widow awaiting legal residency — particularly one handcuffed and held without accommodation for her age or medical needs — represents a disproportionate application of enforcement priorities that damages U.S. relationships with allied nations.

stories.headline: Irish Suspected Crime Boss Daniel Kinahan Arrested in Dubai
stories.summary:
Suspected Irish crime boss Daniel Kinahan was arrested in Dubai on Wednesday, April 15, following a warrant issued by the Irish courts, according to Irish national broadcaster RTÉ and Dubai police. Dubai authorities said the arrest came after intensive search and surveillance operations and was completed within 48 hours of the warrant being issued by Dubai Public Prosecution.

Kinahan, who is in his 40s, is alleged to have played a central role in an international organized crime network. Dubai police said they acted after receiving a judicial file from Irish authorities detailing his alleged crimes and involvement in the criminal organization. The arrest was made in accordance with a bilateral extradition agreement between Ireland and the United Arab Emirates.

An Garda Síochána, the Irish national police, confirmed awareness of the arrest of an Irish national in Dubai and described it as 'another extremely important demonstration of the need for international law enforcement co-operation in tackling transnational organised crime.' The statement indicated that the matter currently remains with UAE authorities as extradition procedures are initiated.

Kinahan has long been one of Europe's most wanted suspected organized crime figures. His arrest marks a significant development in years-long international efforts to bring him to justice, and is being closely watched by law enforcement agencies across Europe and beyond.

stories.headline: Hollywood Studios Preview Avengers: Doomsday, Top Gun 3 and More at CinemaCon
stories.summary:
Major Hollywood studios showcased upcoming films at the annual CinemaCon gathering in Las Vegas this week, offering cinema owners and fans previews of some of the most anticipated releases of the next two years. Disney unveiled the first full trailer for Avengers: Doomsday, scheduled for release on December 18, featuring Robert Downey Jr. returning to the Marvel universe — not as Iron Man but as the villain Doctor Doom.

The trailer also revealed the return of Chris Evans as Steve Rogers and Patrick Stewart reprising his role as Professor Xavier, signaling a broad reassembly of Marvel characters. Evans told the CinemaCon crowd he agreed to return only because 'there is a very real reason that these heroes need Steve Rogers' in the new film.

Paramount announced that Top Gun 3 is officially in development with a script underway, with Tom Cruise set to reprise his role. The sequel follows 2022's Top Gun: Maverick, which earned $1.5 billion at the global box office. Cruise also previewed an unrelated dramatic film, Digger, at the conference earlier in the week.

Other highlights included a preview of Christopher Nolan's adaptation of Homer's The Odyssey, starring Matt Damon, Tom Holland, Anne Hathaway and Zendaya, as well as Focker In-Law, a new installment of the Meet the Parents franchise featuring Ariana Grande alongside Ben Stiller and Robert De Niro.

stories.headline: World Court Marks 80th Anniversary as U.N. Chief Warns of Eroding International Law
stories.summary:
The International Court of Justice marked its 80th anniversary Friday in The Hague, with U.N. Secretary-General António Guterres warning that international law is eroding before the world's eyes. Speaking in the ornate Great Hall before gathered dignitaries — including Dutch King Willem-Alexander — Guterres declared that "the force of law must always prevail over the law of force."

The ceremony stood in sharp contrast to mounting strains on the global legal order. U.S. President Donald Trump has threatened to destroy bridges and power plants in Iran, actions that some military law experts say could constitute war crimes. Russia continues to attack Ukraine in defiance of a standing ICJ order, and Sudan has entered its fourth year of civil war between military and paramilitary forces.

Presiding ICJ judge Yuji Iwasawa said the court responds to contemporary challenges by continuing to interpret and apply international law "rigorously and in good faith." The court is currently at its busiest point in history, handling cases involving the Gaza conflict, the war in Ukraine, climate change, and long-standing border disputes such as the Guyana-Venezuela territorial case dating to 1899.

Oxford international law expert Janina Dill told the Associated Press that "powerful states have thrown out the rule book and perform disdain for international law." The court is also considering genocide allegations against both Myanmar, over its treatment of the Rohingya minority, and Israel, over its military operations in Gaza — charges both countries deny.

stories.headline: China Accuses Japan of Stirring 'Trouble' with Warship Transit of Taiwan Strait
stories.summary:
China on Friday accused Japan of provocative behavior after a Japanese Self-Defense Forces vessel transited the Taiwan Strait, signaling continued strain between Asia's two largest economies. Beijing's objection marks a notable escalation in rhetoric directed at Tokyo over navigation in the sensitive waterway, which separates mainland China from Taiwan.

The Taiwan Strait has become an increasingly contested corridor, with the United States and its allies conducting regular transits that Washington describes as lawful exercises of freedom of navigation. China claims sovereignty over Taiwan and has consistently objected to foreign military vessels passing through the strait, though international law permits such transits in international waters.

Japan's decision to send a naval vessel through the strait is seen as a deliberate signal of alignment with U.S. and allied positions on freedom of navigation in the Indo-Pacific. Tokyo and Beijing have experienced repeated friction in recent years over territorial disputes in the East China Sea and differing postures on Taiwan.

The incident adds a new dimension to regional tensions at a time when global attention is focused on the Iran conflict and U.S.-China diplomatic maneuvering over sanctions and weapons transfers. It also comes as Japan has significantly expanded its defense spending and adopted a more assertive regional security posture.

stories.headline: Angola on Track for $400 Million Debt-for-Education Swap by June
stories.summary:
Angola's Finance Minister Vera Daves de Sousa said Friday that the country is on track to conclude a debt-for-education swap valued at approximately $400 million by June. The deal would convert a portion of Angola's sovereign debt obligations into commitments to fund domestic education programs, a financing mechanism increasingly used by developing nations to address both fiscal pressures and social investment needs.

Debt-for-development swaps allow creditor nations or institutions to forgive or restructure a portion of a borrowing country's debt in exchange for verifiable spending on agreed social or environmental priorities. Angola, one of sub-Saharan Africa's largest oil producers, has carried a heavy debt load accumulated in part through borrowing from China and multilateral lenders during periods of low oil prices.

The announcement reflects broader efforts by African governments to negotiate creative debt relief arrangements as many countries continue to face elevated borrowing costs and constrained fiscal space following the COVID-19 pandemic. Education-linked swaps have gained traction as donors and creditors seek mechanisms that channel savings into measurable human development outcomes.

The timeline of June gives Angola approximately six weeks to finalize terms. No details were immediately available on which creditors are involved in the swap or which specific education programs would be funded under the arrangement.

stories.headline: Update: Iran Opens Strait of Hormuz to Commercial Shipping, Oil Prices Plunge
stories.summary:
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi declared Friday that the Strait of Hormuz is "completely open" to commercial vessels for the duration of the current ceasefire, triggering an immediate and sharp drop in global oil prices. Brent crude fell to $88 per barrel from above $98 earlier in the day — a significant retreat from the March peak of more than $119 per barrel, though still well above pre-conflict levels below $70.

Global equity markets rallied on the announcement. The S&P 500 rose 0.8 percent in early trading, the Nasdaq and Dow Jones Industrial Average each climbed more than 1 percent, and major European indices including the CAC and DAX gained more than 2 percent. The opening of the strait, which carries roughly one-fifth of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas, eases immediate fears of sustained energy shortages that had pushed up petrol, diesel, and jet fuel prices and threatened food supply chains reliant on fertilizer shipments through the waterway.

President Trump welcomed the move on Truth Social, writing that Iran had agreed "to never close the Strait of Hormuz again," but he simultaneously confirmed that a U.S. naval blockade of Iran will remain "in full force and effect" until a permanent deal ending the conflict is reached. Iran's announcement followed a ceasefire agreement between Israel and Lebanon and comes as Trump said U.S.-Iran talks could resume as early as this weekend.

Despite the announcement, at least one oil and gas shipping operator told the BBC the declaration "doesn't change anything" immediately, reflecting uncertainty about operational safety and enforcement. The Strait has been effectively closed since the U.S. and Israel launched military strikes on Iran in late February, and the practical reopening of shipping lanes will depend on conditions on the ground.

stories.headline: Iranian Footballers Speak Out After Being Granted Asylum in Australia
stories.summary:
Two Iranian women's footballers who were granted asylum in Australia have issued their first public statement, saying the country has given them "hope for a future where we can live and compete in safety." Atefeh Ramezanisadeh and Fatemeh Pasandideh were among seven members of the Iranian national team who initially sought humanitarian visas while in Australia for the Women's Asian Cup, though the other five subsequently returned to Iran.

The pair said their primary focus is on safety, health, and rebuilding their lives, thanking the Iranian diaspora community and the Australian government for providing a "safe haven." Both players have been pictured training with A-League Women's club Brisbane Roar and expressed hope to continue their elite sporting careers in Australia.

The players' decision to remain abroad came after footage emerged of a host on Iranian state television calling the team "traitors" who should be punished for not singing the national anthem at their opening match. Human rights activists have said the five players who returned to Iran may have done so under pressure from threats against their families. Iran's sports ministry claimed it had "defeated the enemy's plans," while a news agency affiliated with the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps alleged the players had faced "psychological warfare" and "seductive offers" during their time in Australia.

The episode unfolded against the backdrop of the war in Iran that began on February 28 following U.S.-Israeli air strikes, which has raised fears about the safety of Iranians perceived as insufficiently loyal to the government.

stories.headline: South Korea's Runaway Wolf Neukgu Captured After Nine-Day Search
stories.summary:
Neukgu, a two-year-old wolf who escaped from a zoo in Daejeon, South Korea, was captured early Friday after a nine-day search that captivated the nation. Authorities caught the animal near an expressway at 00:44 local time, tranquilizing him with a dart from about 20 meters away and monitoring his recovery via drone before transporting him back to O-World zoo and theme park.

Hundreds of rescue officials had been deployed during the search, and Neukgu had narrowly evaded capture on multiple occasions, including an incident earlier in the week when he was spotted on a mountain near the zoo before slipping away again. A medical examination found his pulse and body temperature were normal, and authorities said he had not lost much weight. An endoscopic procedure was performed to remove a fishing hook found in his stomach.

Neukgu's escape drew widespread public attention in South Korea, with President Lee Jae Myung publicly calling for his safe return and the wolf inspiring a meme coin billed by creators as a "symbol of independence." Animal rights groups had expressed concern that he could be killed during capture, as happened to a puma that escaped from the same zoo in 2018.

Neukgu is part of a program at O-World to raise awareness of the Korean wolf, a subspecies now considered extinct in the wild on the Korean Peninsula. The Daejeon city government thanked rescue workers and the public in a social media post following the successful operation.

stories.headline: Rep. Luna Accuses Sen. Gallego of Sexual Misconduct and Campaign Finance Violations; Gallego Denies All Allegations
stories.summary:
Republican Rep. Anna Paulina Luna of Florida publicly named Democratic Sen. Ruben Gallego of Arizona as the previously unnamed senator she had accused of "very disturbing" conduct, urging Senate Majority Leader John Thune to open an investigation. The allegations include sexual misconduct and campaign finance violations. Luna made the accusations directly to CBS News, marking a significant escalation in what had been an anonymous charge.

Gallego has denied all wrongdoing. A spokesperson for the Arizona senator dismissed the accusations, stating: "These are right wing conspiracy theories being parroted by a fringe far right member of Congress." No independent corroboration of the allegations has been reported.

The accusations inject fresh controversy into the Senate, where Gallego has been viewed as a rising figure in the Democratic Party following his 2024 Senate win in Arizona. Luna, a prominent member of the House GOP's more confrontational wing, has called on Republican Senate leadership to take the matter seriously.

The episode highlights ongoing tensions between House Republicans and Democratic senators heading into the 2026 midterm cycle, with both parties engaged in aggressive political maneuvering. No formal ethics investigation has been announced as of Friday.

stories.headline: Three Charged with Arson at Persian-Language Media Offices in London
stories.summary:
Three individuals — Oisin McGuinness, 21, Nathan Dunn, 19, both from Watford, and a 16-year-old boy from north London — have been charged with arson with intent to endanger life following an attempted attack on the offices of Volant Media, a Persian-language broadcaster, in the Park Royal area of northwest London on Wednesday evening. An ignited container was thrown at the building but landed in a car park where the fire quickly went out, causing no injuries or significant damage.

All three are British nationals. McGuinness faces an additional charge of dangerous driving after police pursued a black SUV that crashed on Ballards Lane in Finchley. The suspects are due to appear at Westminster Magistrates' Court.

The attempted attack follows a string of recent incidents in London targeting Jewish and Iranian community institutions. Separately, a man and a woman were arrested after petrol-filled bottles were thrown at Finchley Reform Synagogue early Wednesday, and five people were arrested earlier this month after four Jewish charity-owned ambulances were set ablaze in north London. Counter-terrorism police are leading related investigations.

Authorities have not yet confirmed a specific motive for the Volant Media attack, though the cluster of incidents has heightened concern among minority communities in the capital. An Iran-linked Islamist group previously claimed responsibility for some of the London arson attacks currently under counter-terrorism review.

stories.headline: Mugabe's Son Pleads Guilty to Pointing Firearm and Illegal Entry in South Africa
stories.summary:
Bellarmine Mugabe, 28, the youngest son of Zimbabwe's late former president Robert Mugabe, pleaded guilty Friday in a Johannesburg court to pointing a firearm and being illegally present in South Africa. The charges stem from an incident in February in which a 23-year-old man, believed to be a security guard, was shot twice in the back at Mugabe's upmarket Hyde Park home and hospitalized in critical condition.

Mugabe reached a deal with prosecutors, and the National Prosecuting Authority did not confirm what became of the original attempted murder charge he had faced. Co-accused Tobias Matonhodze, 33, pleaded guilty to attempted murder, defeating the ends of justice, illegal immigration, and possession of ammunition. Both men's lawyers indicated their clients were willing to return to Zimbabwe voluntarily if no custodial sentence is imposed.

Sentencing has been postponed to April 24. The court was told the shooting followed a dispute inside the property, with the victim fleeing when he was shot. Authorities have not yet recovered the weapon used.

The case is the latest legal trouble for Bellarmine Mugabe, who has previously been arrested in Zimbabwe for assaulting a police officer and a security guard. His father ruled Zimbabwe for 37 years before being ousted in a 2017 coup and died in 2019.

stories.headline: Acting ICE Director Todd Lyons Resigns, to Depart Agency at End of May
stories.summary:
Acting Immigration and Customs Enforcement Director Todd Lyons submitted his resignation Thursday, with Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin confirming he will leave the agency on May 31 to move to the private sector. Lyons has led ICE since March 2025, overseeing the agency during a period of aggressive immigration enforcement under President Trump's mass deportation campaign.

Lyons's tenure was marked by significant scrutiny, most notably over the fatal shootings of U.S. citizens Renee Good and Alex Pretti in Minneapolis in January. He repeatedly defended ICE's conduct before Congress and in courtrooms as the agency faced mounting legal and political challenges.

Mullin praised Lyons in a statement, saying "thanks to his leadership, American communities are safer." Lyons cited a desire to spend more time with his family and noted he had experienced firsthand the surge in threats against ICE officers during his tenure.

The departure leaves a leadership vacuum at one of the Trump administration's most prominent enforcement agencies at a time when immigration policy remains one of the most divisive political issues in the country. No successor has been publicly named.

stories.headline: France Detains Key Suspect in 1982 Paris Jewish Restaurant Attack After Palestinian Authority Extradition
stories.summary:
French authorities detained a 72-year-old man identified as Hicham Harb, also known as Mahmoud Khader Abed Adra, on Thursday after the Palestinian National Authority extradited him in response to a French request filed last September. Harb is considered a key suspect in the 1982 grenade and machine-gun attack on the Jo Goldenberg restaurant in Paris's historic Marais quarter, which killed six people and wounded more than 20 others.

Harb is suspected of directing the attack and acting as one of the gunmen who fired on diners. No one has ever been convicted for the killings. The attack was attributed to a splinter Palestinian militant group founded by Abu Nidal, whose organization was responsible for approximately 900 deaths across multiple countries during the 1980s.

French President Emmanuel Macron thanked the Palestinian Authority for cooperating, calling it "a concrete demonstration" of judicial cooperation that followed France's recognition of Palestinian statehood in September 2025. French Foreign Minister Jean-Noël Barrot said the extradition meant justice could finally be served 44 years after the attack.

Harb's son Bilal al-Adra called the extradition illegal and said the family had no confidence his father would receive a fair trial. The Paris courts have rejected an appeal to move the case to a jury trial, meaning it will be heard by judges in a specialized anti-terrorism court. Two other suspects are already in France, while three others remain abroad.

stories.headline: Severe Storm System Threatens Over 51 Million Across Midwest and Plains Friday Into Weekend
stories.summary:
A major severe weather system is forecast to affect more than 51 million people from Texas to Wisconsin on Friday evening and into the weekend, bringing threats of strong tornadoes, large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding. The National Weather Service identified the Upper Mississippi Valley — spanning parts of Illinois, Iowa, Minnesota and Wisconsin — as having the highest probability of strong tornadoes, while the central U.S. and Midwest face the greatest risk of very large hail.

The new threat arrives as communities across the region are still recovering from storms earlier in the week. Michigan is experiencing record flooding, with Governor Gretchen Whitmer declaring a state of emergency for 33 counties. Authorities are monitoring dangerously high water levels near a dam in Cheboygan, and the Muskegon River has reached record highs.

In western New York, a community south of Buffalo sustained significant tornado damage Wednesday, while two tornadoes struck Clinton, Missouri on the same night. A 41-year-old man was killed by a suspected lightning strike in Wisconsin, and Milwaukee roads remained flooded Thursday following Wednesday's torrential rain.

Damaging winds reaching up to 70 mph are forecast across a corridor from Texas to Green Bay. The severe weather threat is expected to extend into Saturday, affecting major cities including Cincinnati, Cleveland, Pittsburgh and Buffalo before a cold front moves east and brings temperatures closer to seasonal norms early next week.

stories.headline: Update: Finance Ministers and Top Bankers Raise Serious Concerns About Anthropic's Mythos AI Model
stories.summary:
Finance ministers, central bankers, and financiers have raised urgent concerns about Anthropic's Claude Mythos AI model, warning it could undermine the security of global financial systems. The model has demonstrated an unprecedented ability to identify and exploit cybersecurity vulnerabilities, prompting crisis meetings among top officials. Canadian Finance Minister François-Philippe Champagne confirmed the issue was extensively discussed at the International Monetary Fund meeting in Washington this week, calling the threat an "unknown, unknown" unlike physical chokepoints such as the Strait of Hormuz.

Anthropec has not publicly released Mythos, instead making it available only to select technology companies — including Amazon Web Services, CrowdStrike, Microsoft, and Nvidia — through an initiative called Project Glasswing, described as an effort to secure critical software infrastructure. On Thursday, Anthropic separately released a new version of its existing Claude Opus model, allowing Mythos' cyber capabilities to be tested in less powerful systems.

The UK's AI Security Institute, which was granted access to a preview version, published the only independent assessment of the model. Its researchers found Mythos capable of exploiting systems with weak security but concluded it was not dramatically more capable than its predecessor, Opus 4. Some cybersecurity experts have cautioned that broader alarm may be premature given the model has not been widely tested by the industry.

The episode underscores growing tensions between the rapid development of highly capable AI systems and the ability of regulators and financial institutions to assess and manage the risks they pose. Anthropic's Project Glasswing represents an unusual attempt by an AI developer to proactively engage critical infrastructure operators before a public release.

stories.headline: France and Germany Split on Europe's Role in Securing Strait of Hormuz
stories.summary:
France and Germany are diverging over how Europe should conduct a potential mission to secure shipping lanes in the Strait of Hormuz, as the Iran conflict continues to disrupt global maritime trade. The disagreement emerged ahead of a meeting convened Friday by French President Emmanuel Macron and UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer, bringing together European leaders to discuss a coordinated response to the crisis.

The split reflects broader tensions within Europe over how deeply the continent should become militarily entangled in a conflict involving U.S. and Israeli forces against Iran. France has historically favored a more assertive European defense posture, while Germany has been more cautious about committing forces to Middle Eastern engagements, particularly as its new coalition government navigates domestic political pressures.

The Strait of Hormuz remains a critical artery for global energy supplies, and its continued disruption has already sent fuel prices higher across Europe and strained airline operations. German carriers separately urged their government Thursday to release strategic kerosene reserves to prevent flight cuts ahead of the summer season.

The Macron-Starmer summit represents Europe's most concrete diplomatic effort yet to define its role in the conflict, though the Franco-German rift suggests any coordinated European naval mission faces significant political obstacles before it can be assembled.

stories.headline: Indian Factory Workers' Protests Escalate in Noida as Police Use Tear Gas, Arrest Hundreds
stories.summary:
Large-scale protests by factory workers have escalated in Noida, a major industrial hub near India's capital Delhi, with police deploying tear gas and arresting more than 300 people this week. Thousands of mostly non-unionized contract workers blocked roads demanding higher wages and improved working conditions, in one of the most significant labor uprisings in northern India in recent years. The workers, many of them migrants earning between 10,000 and 15,000 rupees per month, say their wages have remained stagnant for years despite rising living costs.

The unrest was partly triggered by neighboring Haryana state's recent 35 percent minimum wage increase following similar demonstrations there. The government of Uttar Pradesh, where Noida is located, announced a temporary wage increase in two districts and promised further measures, but many workers say the concessions fall short. Labor experts note that minimum wage enforcement in India is inconsistent, with wide state-by-state variation and frequent delays in scheduled revisions.

The protests have taken on a political dimension. Uttar Pradesh Chief Minister Yogi Adityanath characterized the violence as a "conspiracy" to undermine the state's economic development, while opposition leader Rahul Gandhi publicly backed the workers, accusing the government of siding with employers. The absence of major trade union leadership organizing the demonstrations has been noted as unusual for India's labor movement.

The Noida protests highlight structural vulnerabilities in India's manufacturing sector at a moment when the country is positioning itself as an alternative global supply chain hub. Analysts warn that unresolved wage grievances and weak labor enforcement could undermine investor confidence in key industrial corridors.

stories.headline: Update: Pope Leo XIV Escalates 'Masters of War' Rhetoric in Cameroon, Deepening Rift With Trump
stories.summary:
Pope Leo XIV sharpened his condemnation of world leaders who fund wars at the expense of humanitarian needs during a visit Thursday to Bamenda, Cameroon, the epicenter of a separatist conflict that has killed at least 6,000 people. The Pope declared that "the masters of war pretend not to know that it takes only a moment to destroy, yet often a lifetime is not enough to rebuild," and criticized those who "turn a blind eye to the fact that billions of dollars are spent on killing and devastation" while resources for healing and education remain scarce.

The remarks represent a direct escalation from earlier statements that already drew a sharp public rebuke from President Trump, who posted on Truth Social that "Leo should get his act together as Pope" after the pontiff called the U.S.-Israeli military operation against Iran "atrocious." The Pope had previously expressed concern about Trump's warning that "a whole civilisation will die" if Iran refused U.S. demands. Leo told reporters at the start of his Africa tour that he did not wish to debate Trump but would continue to promote peace.

The Pope's Cameroon address also addressed the local Anglophone separatist crisis, condemning "an endless cycle of destabilisation and death" and accusing outside actors of extracting regional resources while investing profits in weapons. The Archbishop of Canterbury, Sarah Mullally, said she stood with the Pope in his "courageous call for a kingdom of peace."

The widening public dispute between the Vatican and the White House is without modern precedent in its directness. It places Leo — the first U.S.-born Pope — in open rhetorical conflict with a sitting American president over a live military conflict, raising questions about the diplomatic role the Catholic Church may seek to play as U.S.-Iran negotiations continue ahead of the April 22 deadline.

stories.headline: Singer D4vd Arrested on Suspicion of Murdering Teenage Girl
stories.summary:
Los Angeles police arrested 21-year-old singer D4vd, whose legal name is David Anthony Burke, on Thursday on suspicion of murdering 14-year-old Celeste Rivas Hernandez, who went missing in April 2024. Burke is being held without bail, and the case is set to be presented to the District Attorney's office on Monday. The arrest marks the first significant development in a case that has gripped public attention for months.

Hernandez's decomposed remains were discovered in September 2024 in the front trunk of a Tesla registered to D4vd's Texas address at a Hollywood tow yard. Authorities received reports of a foul odor from the vehicle, and investigators found her head and torso inside a cadaver bag. The county medical examiner deferred ruling on the cause of death pending further investigation, and a judge sealed the teen's death records in November.

A grand jury began hearing evidence in the case in December. Additional details emerged after D4vd's father, Dawud Burke, fought a California court summons in Texas, inadvertently releasing previously sealed case materials. The filing confirmed D4vd had been identified in connection with the case.

Burke's attorneys pushed back firmly against the arrest, stating: "Actual evidence in this case will show that David Burke did not murder Celeste Rivas Hernandez and he was not the cause of her death." They also noted that no indictment has been returned by a grand jury and no criminal complaint has been filed, characterizing Burke's detention as being held only "under suspicion."

stories.headline: Iran-Linked Islamist Group Claims London Arson Attacks; Counter-Terrorism Police Lead Investigation
stories.summary:
An Islamist group calling itself Harakat Ashab al-Yamin al-Islamiya — The Islamic Movement of the People of the Right Hand — has claimed responsibility for three separate attacks in north London, posting videos on social media. The unsubstantiated claims come after an attempted arson at a Persian-language media organization in Park Royal on Wednesday evening, as well as separate attacks on a synagogue in Finchley and four ambulances belonging to a Jewish charity in Golders Green. Scotland Yard's counter-terrorism unit is leading the investigation, though none of the incidents has been formally declared a terrorist act.

Police said an ignited container was thrown toward the Volant Media building in Park Royal at around 20:30 BST Wednesday but landed in a car park where the fire quickly went out. Officers subsequently pursued a black SUV until it crashed in Finchley, leading to the arrest of a 16-year-old boy and two men aged 19 and 21 on suspicion of arson endangering life. All three remain in custody.

Authorities have made a total of eight arrests across the three incidents. Three people have already been charged in connection with the Golders Green ambulance attack. Deputy Commissioner Matt Jukes said investigators are keeping "an open mind regarding motivation" given the nature of the locations targeted and similarities between the offences.

Officials issued a pointed warning to individuals being recruited to act on behalf of foreign states. Deputy Assistant Commissioner Vicki Evans said the group is a known entity and that its alleged involvement is "one of our many lines of inquiry." The Metropolitan Police also cautioned that individuals acting "out of hatred, racism or antisemitism" would face prosecution.

stories.headline: Update: Pope Leo XIV Presses 'Masters of War' Theme in Cameroon, Escalating Rift with Trump
stories.summary:
Pope Leo XIV delivered a forceful address in Bamenda, Cameroon on Thursday, condemning leaders who spend "billions of dollars on killing and devastation" while neglecting resources for healing and education. The remarks represent an escalation of the pontiff's public rhetoric against war and come just days after a high-profile clash with U.S. President Donald Trump, who attacked the Pope on Truth Social after Leo criticized U.S. threats against Iran. The Pope said he would not engage in debate with Trump but would continue advocating for peace.

Speaking in a region at the center of a nearly decade-long separatist insurgency that has killed at least 6,000 people, Leo condemned what he called "an endless cycle of destabilisation and death," accusing those who extract the region's resources of reinvesting profits in weapons. He warned that "the masters of war pretend not to know that it takes only a moment to destroy, yet often a lifetime is not enough to rebuild."

The speech deepens the rift between the Vatican and the Trump administration over the U.S.-Israeli military campaign against Iran. Trump had posted that Leo should "get his act together as Pope" after the Pope called the conflict "atrocious" and said Jesus could not be used to justify war. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth had also drawn Vatican criticism after reciting a prayer at a Pentagon service referencing "overwhelming violence."

The Archbishop of Canterbury, Sarah Mullally, expressed solidarity with the Pope following Thursday's address, saying she stood with his "courageous call for a kingdom of peace." Leo, who became the first U.S.-born pope last year, has previously clashed with the Trump administration on immigration policy as well.

stories.headline: UK Prime Minister Starmer Says He Was Not Told Ambassador Mandelson Failed Security Vetting
stories.summary:
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said Friday it is "unforgivable" that he was not informed Peter Mandelson had failed security vetting before being appointed as the UK's ambassador to the United States. Starmer told reporters he had previously assured Parliament that due process had been followed in Mandelson's appointment, only to discover that neither he nor any minister was informed of the vetting failure. He pledged to present a full account of the scandal to Parliament on Monday.

"Not only was I not told, no minister was told, and I'm absolutely furious about that," Starmer said in a broadcast interview. The disclosure represents a significant political embarrassment for the Labour government, which has faced scrutiny over the appointment of Mandelson, a veteran political operator and former EU trade commissioner, to one of the UK's most prominent diplomatic posts.

The scandal raises questions about the integrity of the vetting and appointment process within the British civil service and Cabinet Office, as well as the flow of sensitive security information to elected ministers. Starmer's admission that Parliament was given an inaccurate picture of the vetting process puts pressure on the government to provide a transparent accounting of how the oversight occurred.

Mandelson has been a controversial figure in British politics for decades and his selection as ambassador was already subject to scrutiny given the sensitivity of UK-U.S. relations under the Trump administration. The government has not yet indicated what consequences, if any, Mandelson will face as a result of the vetting revelation.

stories.headline: Judge Halts Aboveground Construction of Trump's White House Ballroom
stories.summary:
A federal judge has blocked the aboveground portion of President Trump's proposed White House ballroom project, ruling that Congress must approve the construction. Judge Richard Leon wrote that the administration appeared to be attempting to circumvent a prior court order by reclassifying the ballroom plans as a national security necessity, stating that "national security is not a blank cheque to proceed with otherwise unlawful activity." The judge did allow underground construction to continue.

The Justice Department filed a notice of appeal shortly after the ruling. Trump responded on Truth Social, insisting the ballroom was "needed now" and that "no judge can be allowed to stop" it. He argued the complex — which he says will include bomb shelters, medical facilities, and large-scale meeting space — is "vital for National Security and Military Operations" and that the above- and below-ground elements are inseparably linked.

The lawsuit was brought by the National Trust for Historic Preservation, which argued the White House violated federal law by beginning construction without filing plans with the National Capital Planning Commission, skipping an environmental assessment, and failing to seek congressional authorization. The East Wing, built in 1902, was demolished in October to make way for the project, whose footprint has since expanded from a 500-person ballroom to a venue for 1,350 guests.

The $400 million project, said to be funded entirely by private donors, is one of several controversial Trump efforts to reshape Washington's landscape. Separately on Thursday, a federal panel gave preliminary approval to Trump's proposed 250-foot victory arch in the capital, despite overwhelmingly negative public feedback.

stories.headline: Trump Says He Is 'OK' With Congressional Hearings for Epstein Survivors
stories.summary:
President Trump signaled Thursday that he would not oppose Congress holding public hearings featuring survivors of convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. Speaking to reporters before departing for Las Vegas, Trump said "I'm OK with it" but noted he understood "that the women didn't want to go under oath." The remarks came amid renewed public pressure for greater transparency around the Epstein case and related documents.

Trump also told reporters he had just left a White House meeting on unconfirmed reports of missing or deceased nuclear scientists, calling the matter "pretty serious stuff." He offered no additional details. Questions have circulated in scientific and national security communities about the deaths and disappearances of several nuclear scientists in recent months.

The Epstein issue has remained politically charged across party lines, with members of both parties calling for greater disclosure. Any congressional hearings would likely revisit the circumstances of Epstein's 2019 death in federal custody, which was ruled a suicide but has continued to generate public skepticism. No formal hearing has been scheduled.

stories.headline: Naples Bank Robbers Hold 25 Hostages Then Escape Through Underground Tunnel
stories.summary:
Several armed men robbed a branch of Crédit Agricole in Naples, Italy on Thursday, seizing 25 hostages in a midday operation before escaping through a tunnel, likely into the city's sewer system. Police surrounded the bank shortly after the robbery began around noon local time, and negotiators worked with the robbers over a roughly two-hour standoff before all hostages were released unharmed. Six people required medical attention for shock.

Firefighters and carabinieri special forces, flown in from Tuscany, ultimately breached the bank by smashing a window with battering rams. By that point, however, the robbers had already vanished underground. Investigators were seen peering into a nearby manhole as a large crowd gathered outside. The value of the stolen goods could not be immediately determined, as the robbers targeted personal safety deposit boxes rather than cash.

The dramatic episode drew tens of thousands of viewers on a local livestream and prompted a large security and rescue mobilization across the southern Italian city. No arrests had been reported as of the time of filing.

stories.headline: Anti-French African Activist Kemi Seba Arrested in South Africa While Allegedly Fleeing to Europe
stories.summary:
South African police arrested Kemi Seba, a French-born Beninese activist wanted in Benin on charges of inciting rebellion in connection with a foiled coup attempt last December, on Monday in a sting operation at a shopping center in Pretoria. Seba, whose legal name is Stellio Gilles Robert Capo Chichi, was seized alongside his 18-year-old son and a paid facilitator who was allegedly helping them cross into Zimbabwe en route to Europe. He is also wanted in France for alleged criminal activities related to crimes against the state.

Seba, 45, is a prominent pan-African voice with 1.5 million online followers who is known for opposing French influence in Africa, backing West African military juntas, and has been accused by French officials of functioning as a mouthpiece for Russian propaganda. He was stripped of his French citizenship in 2024, after which he burned his passport publicly. He was subsequently granted a diplomatic passport by Niger's military junta, which designated him a special adviser.

In December 2024, soldiers briefly claimed to have overthrown Benin's government before the attempt was suppressed within hours with assistance from Nigeria and France. Seba had posted a video declaring it "the day of liberation" for Beninese people. His supporters have denounced the current arrest as politically motivated, calling it an attempt to silence a prominent pan-African activist. His case was adjourned to April 20, with extradition proceedings under way.

stories.headline: Democrats Lead Republicans in Early Midterm Fundraising as Q1 FEC Reports Land
stories.summary:
First-quarter campaign finance reports released this week show Democrats posting substantial fundraising advantages in competitive House and Senate races, according to Federal Election Commission filings. Democrats reported large hauls across high-stakes contests, a development party strategists say reflects grassroots enthusiasm heading into what is shaping up to be a fiercely contested midterm cycle.

The filings offer an early snapshot of the financial battlefield as both parties navigate internal generational and ideological pressures. Primary contests are beginning to take shape in several states, and money raised now will influence candidate viability and messaging capacity through the fall. Republican strategists have noted that incumbency advantages and the structural map of Senate seats up for election in 2026 may offset Democratic financial edges.

Control of Congress hangs in the balance, with Democrats seeking to reclaim the House majority and defend or expand their Senate position. The Q1 numbers set an early benchmark but campaign finance experts caution that fundraising at this stage does not reliably predict November outcomes.
